data.content:
## Incremental Build Analysis

Both custom targets in `IncrementalBroken.csproj` break incremental builds:

### 1. `GenerateTimestamp` (line 6–11)
**Always invalidates the build.** It writes `DateTime.Now` to `build-timestamp.txt` before every compile. The content is different on every run, so the output file always changes. It also has no `Inputs`/`Outputs` attributes, so MSBuild can never skip it.

### 2. `EmbedGitHash` (line 13–23)
**Rewrites a source file on every build.** Even when the git hash hasn't changed, `WriteLinesToFile Overwrite="true"` unconditionally rewrites `git-hash.g.cs`, updating its filesystem timestamp. That file is added to `<Compile>`, so `CoreCompile` sees a "newer" input and recompiles everything. Again, no `Inputs`/`Outputs` to allow skipping.

### Root Causes
| Issue | Target | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| No `Inputs`/`Outputs` | Both | MSBuild always executes the target |
| Content changes every run | `GenerateTimestamp` | Output file always differs → downstream rebuild |
| Unconditional `Overwrite="true"` | `EmbedGitHash` | File timestamp updated even when content is identical → triggers `CoreCompile` |

### Recommended Fixes
- **`GenerateTimestamp`**: Remove it, or write once and skip on subsequent builds (a timestamp that changes every build is fundamentally anti-incremental).
- **`EmbedGitHash`**: Write to a `.tmp` file first, then compare with the existing `.g.cs` file using a `Copy` with `SkipUnchangedFiles="true"` or a conditional `Exec` (`fc` / `diff`). Add `Inputs`/`Outputs` so the target is skippable when the git HEAD hasn't changed.
data.reasoningText:
I can see two custom targets that have incremental build issues. Let me analyze them:

1. **`GenerateTimestamp`** (line 6-11): This target runs `BeforeTargets="CoreCompile"` and writes the current timestamp (`$([System.DateTime]::Now.ToString())`) to a file. Since the timestamp changes every time, this file will always have new content, which will cause MSBuild to consider the output changed and trigger a recompile every single build. Additionally, the target has no `Inputs`/`Outputs` attributes, so MSBuild will always run it.

2. **`EmbedGitHash`** (line 13-23): This target also runs `BeforeTargets="CoreCompile"`. It gets the git hash and writes it to a generated `.cs` file. 

The problem is that even though the git hash itself doesn't change unless you commit, the target lacks `Inputs`/`Outputs` attributes so it always executes. And since `WriteLinesToFile` with `Overwrite="true"` always updates the file timestamp regardless of whether the content actually changed, the generated `.cs` file appears modified to MSBuild, forcing `CoreCompile` to re-run unnecessarily.

The core issues are that both targets always execute and always update file timestamps, breaking incremental builds. ` and conditionally write the file only when the content actually changes. Let me break down the core issue here.
